channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Kushner-Linked Luxury Resort Plans Ignite Protests Across Albania
item.description: Thousands call for Tirana to block the investment projects, citing environmental and corruption concerns.

item.title: Meanwhile, in the Western Hemisphere…
item.description: The U.S. military remains extremely active in and around Latin America.

item.title: How Trump Should Approach an Iran Endgame
item.description: Former Iran negotiator Robert Malley on the White House’s best options right now.

item.title: Trump Is Playing With Fire in Cuba
item.description: The U.S. blockade on the island is unprecedented—and could lead to prolonged upheaval.

item.title: China Is Too Big for Shangri-La
item.description: Snubbing a key security forum shows Beijing’s new confidence.

item.title: Trump’s New ‘Forced Labor’ Tariffs Are a Fig Leaf
item.description: The new import duties are “a solution in search of a problem.”

item.title: China Is Not Europe’s Competitiveness Bogeyman
item.description: Calls for European leaders to protect domestic industry from "unfairly" subsidized Chinese competition have lately grown louder. But while tariffs might offer temporary relief to a few sectors, they cannot restore Europe's technological leadership, industrial dynamism, or export competitiveness.

item.title: What India’s Latest Press Freedom Ranking Reveals About Its Democratic Trajectory
item.description: The RSF’s score for India is significant not for the number itself, but for what that ranking reflects about deeper structural trends affecting journalism, media ownership, and democratic accountability.

item.title: Trilateral Dynamics: China’s Strategy to Test US Restraint of Japan
item.description: Takaichi’s overwhelming victory during the February election suggests China’s coercive approach might be counterproductive. But Beijing’s tactics are not aimed only at the bilateral relationship.

item.title: China, India, and the Emerging Green Divide
item.description: Clean technology has become embedded within a wider geopolitical contest over industrial leadership, technological sovereignty, and influence in the world, especially in the Global South.

item.title: Quetta Train Bombing Points to Escalating Insurgent Violence in Balochistan
item.description: The attack, which coincided with Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif’s visit to China, was aimed at sending China a message that its investments in Balochistan are not safe.

item.title: Putting American Science on a MAGA Leash
item.description: A draft rule from US President Donald Trump’s White House Office of Management and Budget would subject all scientific research funding to the whims of political appointees, further undercutting a pillar of American power. The goal is to foreclose any future that is plural, contestable, and beyond the president’s control.

item.title: America’s secretary of war pulls his punches on China
item.description: The Pentagon’s strategy for Asia contains a very big hole

item.title: Worries about migrants imperil South Korea’s shipbuilding boom
item.description: Compared with most rich countries, the foreign workforce is still small

item.title: Sex tourists fuel outrage about vice in Japan
item.description: The debates do not have women’s interests at heart

item.title: The rise of One Nation is melting Australian politics
item.description: Could the tumult produce a new party?

item.title: Ma Ning will proudly represent China at the World Cup
item.description: The referee is succeeding where the men’s national team is not

item.title: Xi Jinping gives China’s crack scientists new jobs inside government
item.description: Tech rivalry is reshaping the ruling elite

item.title: Donald Trump says Pete Hegseth loves war. That should disqualify him
item.description: How did standards for military leaders fall so low?

item.title: Investment in agricultural tech is growing
item.description: Startups are combining AI and genetics to make more food for less money

item.title: Italy has tracked down Cosa Nostra’s riches
item.description: A €200m seizure from the Corleone Mafia that once terrorised the country

item.title: What to read to understand your next employer
item.description: From Kafka’s “The Trial” to Orwell’s “1984”

item.title: Two American tycoons are betting big on a casino revival
item.description: Caesars and MGM may soon be under new ownership

item.title: Lego, Pokémon and the future of fun
item.description: Toys and media brands are increasingly joining forces

item.title: The Gulf’s rulers want to reduce their dependence on Western arms
item.description: A new defence champion is rising up from the region

item.title: The parable of the tshukudu, Goma’s quintessential transport
item.description: War and cheap Chinese tricycles are disrupting a Congolese city’s idiosyncratic scooter

item.title: Gulf rulers want to prove their strongmen chops
item.description: Unable to control external threats, they are squeezing their own people

item.title: Nigeria’s Christian groups scramble to win over Trump’s America
item.description: To help their cause, they are adopting tropes and rhetoric from the American right

item.title: Protesters have controlled Bolivia’s capital for a month
item.description: To lift their blockades, they want the centrist president, Rodrigo Paz, to resign

item.title: A new defence champion is rising from the Gulf
item.description: The region’s rulers want to reduce their dependence on Western arms

item.title: A new defence champion is rising from the Gulf
item.description: The region’s rulers want to reduce their dependence on Western arms

item.title: Gulf rulers are desperate to prove they are in fact strongmen
item.description: Unable to control external threats, they are squeezing their own people

item.title: How to make football more exciting
item.description: The World Cup is wonderful. It could be even better

item.title: Europe needs Ukraine’s help just as badly as the other way round
item.description: The EU should start drafting a full accession treaty now

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
How long can Pedro Sánchez last?
item.description: The Socialists’ mounting scandals threaten to bring down Spain’s prime minister

item.title: Ukraine is not a charity case
item.description: Europe needs its help just as badly as the other way round

item.title: Are We Reliving the 1890s, 1930s, or 1950s?
item.description: What history can and cannot explain about today’s unraveling order.

item.title: Russia’s Dual Afghan Strategy and Pakistan’s Shrinking Room for Maneuver
item.description: Moscow's deepening relations with the Taliban hint at growing doubts about Pakistan as a counterterrorism partner.

item.title: Pete Hegseth’s Dangerous Call to Arms in Asia
item.description: Much like it did in Europe, the Trump administration has called on US allies in the Indo-Pacific to increase military spending to 3.5% of GDP. But a defense buildup on this scale would jeopardize economic growth and regional unity, and could lead Southeast Asian countries to gravitate toward China’s strategic orbit.

item.title: Estonia Has Lessons for Us All
item.description: The small Baltic country has refashioned the relationship between citizens and the state for the current moment.

item.title: The Pope’s AI Vision and Its Limits
item.description: Pope Leo XIV’s encyclical on AI is clear-eyed about the limits of techno-utopianism. But its vision of technology directed toward human flourishing needs to be extended to include the interests of every sentient being, human and nonhuman, biological or—possibly—artificial.

item.title: India’s surprise baby bust is a warning to the world
item.description: It is not just rich places that are becoming less fertile

item.title: Europe is winning the easy half of its migration battle
item.description: Numbers of new arrivals into the EU are markedly lower

item.title: Europe has reduced illegal immigration without goon tactics
item.description: But that is just the easy half of its migration battle

item.title: India’s population will soon be falling—probably quite fast
item.description: Neither widespread poverty, nor high rates of marriage nor relatively young mothers are sustaining fertility

item.title: Pakistan is battling two insurgencies
item.description: Problems mount at home as its leader plays peacemaker abroad

item.title: Indians can now bet on the monsoon
item.description: Heatwaves may be more important

item.title: European electricity markets have too much power
item.description: They must learn to deal with it

item.title: Vladimir Putin’s Second-Biggest Headache
item.description: With his war in Ukraine going badly, he may soon face another quagmire in Chechnya.

item.title: The Iran War Is Fueling a Global Debt Shock
item.description: Once again, a global crisis is disproportionately burdening countries that did not cause it. Across the Global South, the food- and energy-price shock triggered by the conflict in the Middle East has morphed into a debt shock that demands an urgent international response.

item.title: How to crush Gen-Z socialism
item.description: The me-first doctrine is a threat to prosperity

item.title: How to fight back against Gen-Z socialism
item.description: The me-first doctrine is a threat to prosperity

item.title: Some billionaires pay too little tax
item.description: But the case for levies on wealth is unconvincing

item.title: Gen-Z socialism, from Zohran to Zack and beyond
item.description: The world’s leftists are embracing a new set of economic ideas

item.title: Fixing America’s decaying Treasury market
item.description: High debt, disjointed markets and pugnacious trade policy all threaten the world’s safe asset

item.title: America’s decaying Treasury market needs a fix
item.description: High debt, disjointed markets and pugnacious trade policy all threaten the world’s safe asset

item.title: California is on the cusp of its “Becerra era”
item.description: What would Xavier Becerra’s leadership mean for America’s most populous state?

item.title: How Measles Came Roaring Back
item.description: Bangladesh is facing soaring measles cases not because of a single localized failure, but because of years of systemic erosion. Ultimately, maintaining herd immunity against highly contagious viruses requires relentless attention to supply and demand, as well as to the broader social and institutional foundations of public health.

item.title: The Threat of Unrest Is Decarbonizing the Global South
item.description: Oil and gas price spikes are doing what decades of climate diplomacy struggled to achieve.

item.title: Journalism and Fighting for Indigenous Rights in Myanmar
item.description: Scott Johnson talks about drones and meeting Nerdah Bo Mya in Kawthoolei.

item.title: The Transatlantic Crucible
item.description: The crisis between Washington and Europe may be a blessing in disguise.

item.title: The American Military’s Coming Marathon
item.description: The Pentagon needs both quantity and quality to win modern conflicts.

item.title: The Transatlantic Crucible
item.description: The crisis between Washington and Europe may be a blessing in disguise.

item.title: Can ASEAN’s Green Goals Survive Its Data Center Boom?
item.description: Data centers are critical enablers of ASEAN’s digital economy, but rapid digitalization presents a number of serious sustainability challenges.

item.title: To Lam’s Southeast Asia Visits Expand Vietnam’s ‘Neighborhood Bamboo Diplomacy’
item.description: Hanoi's closer engagement with its ASEAN neighbors represents an enhancement of its flexible, omnidirectional foreign policy doctrine.

item.title: Can ‘Compulsory Conciliation’ Resolve the Cambodia-Thailand Maritime Border Dispute?
item.description: Bangkok's recent withdrawal from a 2001 MoU governing maritime border talks has prompted Phnom Penh to internationalize the dispute.

item.title: Major ASEAN Economies In Line For New US Tariffs Over Forced Labor
item.description: U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer has recommended the imposition 10-12.5 percent levies on 60 major trading partners.

item.title: Why Beijing Still Fears the Tiananmen Mothers
item.description: Thirty-seven years later, the regime’s legitimacy still rests on suppressing the truth of what happened on June 4, 1989.

item.title: Nature and Concrete: The Eco-Brutalist Resistance in Central Asia
item.description: Eco-brutalism is an aesthetic appeal to a healthier, greener future, and one that can be built atop existing realities.

item.title: Australia’s Strategic Ambiguity Is Running Out of Road
item.description: Washington wants allies to spend more on defense, while Beijing judges Australia by what it does, not what it says. Canberra needs to take stock of its own interests.

item.title: Tiananmen Is Not Just China’s Story
item.description: The 1989 crackdown reinforced a political order that made independent worker organizing nearly impossible. The effects have been felt across the global economy.
